data.title: GOP warnings over Greenland look unlikely to sway Trump
data.description:
Republican senators are bracing themselves for the potential fallout of President Trump’s aggressive bid to seize control of Greenland as the president prepares to meet with European leaders at the World Economic Forum in Davos, Switzerland, on Wednesday.

Key GOP lawmakers have warned Trump to back away from his aggressive efforts to take control of Greenland, but their efforts have failed to make an impact.

The president over the weekend instead escalated the standoff with NATO allies by threatening steep tariffs on Germany, France, Britain and other European countries unless they move away from their opposition to the U.S. taking Greenland.

Two Republicans who are attending the economic forum in Davos are making it clear to European allies that Trump’s audacious efforts to ramp up pressure on Denmark and other European countries have divided the Republican Party in Washington.

Sen. Thom Tillis (R-N.C.) on Tuesday called Trump’s push for Greenland “a waste of taxpayer dollars” and “drama that we don’t need” during an interview in Davos with CNBC’s “Squawk Box.”

Tillis said Trump needs an adviser who “understands the destabilizing influence on NATO” created by his unrelenting push to take control of Greenland.

Sen. Lisa Murkowski (R-Alaska), who is also attending meetings in Davos, has called Trump’s threats against European allies “deeply troubling.”

She said the tariffs Trump announced against European trading partners were “unnecessary, punitive and a profound mistake.”

Trump is expected to have conversations with NATO Secretary-General Mark Rutte while in Switzerland.

A Senate Republican aide who requested anonymity to comment on reactions from Republican lawmakers to Trump’s latest tariff threats questioned whether the president has the authority under the International Emergency Economic Powers Act to impose tariffs on allies because they have supported Denmark’s refusal to simply cede its territory to the United States.

Greenland has long been a part of Denmark, and its citizens have not indicated a desire to become part of the United States.

The GOP aide argued Trump justified his first round of “Liberation Day” tariffs against Europe last year as being driven by an economic emergency but asserted that Trump’s bid for Greenland “has nothing to do with economics.”

“They sure haven’t laid out a very good case in the court of public opinion,” the aide said, arguing Trump hasn’t made a clear economic argument for taking control of Greenland.

The aide said Republican senators will watch closely to see what impact Trump’s threats are having on the financial markets and what additional steps he takes to achieve his goal of wresting Greenland from Danish control.

“We’ll see how much effect this will have on the market by the time everyone gets back [to Washington] and what practical steps the administration has taken,” the source said.

The major U.S. stock indices dropped by about 2 percent on Tuesday as investors digested Trump’s latest round of tariff threats. It marked the worst day of trading since October.

Trump appears undeterred by the backlash from European allies and some GOP lawmakers in Washington.

John Ullyot, a GOP strategist who served as a deputy assistant to Trump during his first term, said the president has made up his mind and predicted it’s unlikely anything will divert him from his focus on taking Greenland.

“President Trump has made up his mind, and when he makes up his mind, no one can talk him off of it. That’s been very good for him in so many ways politically for the last 10 years,” he said.

Ullyot said past presidents going back to Dwight D. Eisenhower, the nation’s 34th commander in chief, “have thought about the value of Greenland.”

“It’s even more important now with the rare earth [minerals] that weren’t a big factor 50, 70 years ago,” he said. “He put out exactly what he wants to do here and senators who are very pro-NATO are going to have heartburn with this, but it’s going to be hard for them to go against the president.

He predicted a bigger move from Trump to gain control of Greenland is “likely to happen in the short term.”

Ullyot said Trump “has already telegraphed that he’s willing to put the NATO alliance on the edge for wanting to get Greenland.”

Whit Ayres, a prominent Republican pollster, offered a different view. He said Trump’s all-out push to acquire Greenland has little public support in the United States and has divided the Republican Party.

“Based on the polling I’ve seen on the Greenland gambit, Sen. Tillis succinctly summed up public opinion when he said, ‘I’m sick of stupid.’ The support for aggressively going after Denmark to take control of Greenland has the support of maybe half of Trump’s base, at best,” Ayres said.

He noted that a recent Reuters/Ipsos poll found that just 4 percent of surveyed Americans, including just 1 in 10 Republicans, think it would be a “good idea” to use military force to take possession of Greenland.  

“You can get 4 percent of Americans in a survey to say they believe little green men live on the moon,” he quipped.

“Rarely does the public so widely agree on a foreign policy question as they agree on this Greenland gambit,” Ayres added, observing that a foreign policy issue has rarely been so elevated in the national political debate since the end of the Iraq War.

A second Senate GOP aide who requested anonymity to comment on sentiment within the GOP conference over Trump’s Greenland agenda said a lot will depend on whether the president succeeds in cowing allies.

The aide, however, said many Republicans aren’t happy about Trump renewing the trade war with European economic powers after tariff rates appeared to be settled after unveiling a deal in late July.

European leaders are now discussing the implementation of $108 billion in retaliatory tariffs on American products. Most worrisome for the U.S. stock markets, Europeans are mulling significant restrictions on American Big Tech companies, a drastic tactic that has been dubbed a trade “bazooka.”

Apple, which has significant business interests in Europe, saw its share price drop by 4 percent Tuesday.

Former Republican Leader Mitch McConnell (Ky.) is one of the loudest critics of Trump’s brinkmanship with NATO allies over Greenland.

He warned on the Senate floor that undermining NATO would harm U.S. national security interests far more than acquiring Greenland would help them. He said that “incinerating the hard-won trust of loyal allies” would result in an “unprecedented act of strategic self-harm.”

Former Sen. Judd Gregg (N.H.), who served on McConnell’s Senate GOP leadership team, said more Republican senators need to speak out against Trump’s aggression toward Greenland and NATO allies.

“A lot of them appear to be hiding under their desks, which is inexcusable. They should be speaking up aggressively and saying this is a terrible idea and it undermines our position with key allies,” he said of GOP senators who have shied from confronting Trump over his posture on Greenland, despite their personal misgivings.

Gregg pointed out that Denmark is willing to expand the U.S. military presence on Greenland to deter Chinese and Russian expansion into the Arctic, a point several sitting Republican senators have made over the past week.

“The fact is over the years we’ve gotten whatever we want in the way of military cooperation from Denmark and from Greenland. In fact, for years we had many bases there,” he said. “It’s not a military issue.”

Gregg said the president “claims it’s for bases, it’s really for his ego.”

He said Trump “likes to be the center of attention and he likes to stir things up.”

Gregg argued the fight with NATO allies over Greenland threatens to “undermine a lot of people’s confidence in American leadership on other issues, whether it’s trade or international authority.”

data.title: 5 things to know about Virginia's redistricting effort
data.description:
Virginia Democrats are moving to pass a new House map that will allow them to net a handful of seats ahead of the November midterms.

Democrats in the Old Dominion passed a constitutional amendment for a second time last week to allow the party to redraw the congressional lines by 2030.

The move comes as Florida Republicans are planning to convene in April for a special redistricting session, underscoring the urgency both parties face ahead of an election that will decide control of the House and Senate for President Trump’s last two years in office.

Here are five things to know about Virginia’s redistricting effort:

The decision will go before voters

The constitutional amendment passed by Virginia Democrats will now go before voters, allowing them to decide whether lawmakers can do mid-decade redistricting under a narrow set of circumstances.

The constitutional amendment would allow legislators to redraw Virginia’s map by October 2030 if another state has conducted mid-decade redistricting for any other reason besides responding to a court order or finishing the decennial redistricting process after the 2020 U.S. Census. It also would allow Democrats to temporarily bypass their redistricting commission.

Lawmakers passed the constitutional amendment twice — before and after the 2025 elections — and Democrats are eyeing an April statewide vote on the measure. Democrats also said they plan to have a congressional map finalized before the special election so voters can see what kind of map they’re weighing.

Democrats need a simple majority in the election to pass the constitutional amendment before the new maps can be put in place.

The map hasn’t been decided yet

Virginia Democrats are considering two different maps as the party weighs how aggressive it should be ahead of the midterms.

Democrats have a 6-5 edge in the state’s congressional delegation, but the party is eyeing either a 9-2 favored map or a 10-1 map. Some top Democrats have signaled they’re planning for the more aggressive 10-1 map.

“I said in August of 2025 that the maps will be 10-1 and I’m sticking with that today. Anyone in the Congressional delegation who wants a seat needs to campaign for it and not expect a safe seat,” Virginia Senate President Pro Tem L. Louise Lucas (D) wrote earlier this month.

Democrats have an incentive to pass a more aggressive map given that Florida Republicans are eying new maps, which could net up to five seats for the GOP. Yet it also creates more competitive congressional districts for some Democrats by expanding the map.

Democrats, however, are expected to see a favorable midterm environment in November, which could ease some of those concerns.

The party plans to decide which map it wants to use by the end of January. The candidate filing deadline for Virginia’s June primary is April 2, meaning Democrats will need to adjust the date in order for candidates to qualify under the new lines.

Democrats expect the effort to pass

Democrats feel confident that their redistricting measure will pass given Virginia’s blue leanings statewide.

Though there has been little public polling over redistricting in the Old Dominion, the party has been able to successfully convince voters to approve mid-decade redistricting elsewhere.

California Democrats were able to overwhelmingly pass their new House map, which aims to net the party up to five seats, in November. The party benefited from an unorganized and insufficiently funded opposition campaign in the Golden State.

Meanwhile, Virginia Democrats are coming out of a successful election in 2025, which saw them overperform in state legislative races, as well as the contest for governor.

GOP has sought to halt the effort

Republicans have tried unsuccessfully to halt Democrats’ effort to pass their redistricting measure multiple times. Most recently, a county judge last week denied a bid by the GOP to block the redistricting process.

“For well over a century, the courts of the Commonwealth have recognized a bedrock principle that amending the Constitution is a process left exclusively to the sound judgment of the Legislature that proposes amendments and the citizens that ratify or reject them,” the judge wrote.

“While that process is ongoing, ‘the courts cannot interfere to stop any of the proceedings,’” the judge added.

Republicans are expected to continue fighting the redistricting process, likely through an opposition campaign organized against the ballot measure. However, the party could try to thwart the process in order ways, such as by contesting the ballot language.

Republicans faced a similar setback in California after a federal panel of judges rejected a bid last week to halt the Golden State’s new congressional map from going into effect before November.

Democrats were also unsuccessful at barring Texas from using its new GOP-favored map in time for the 2026 midterms.

It’s Dems’ last big opportunity to gain seats

The stakes of Virginia’s redistricting effort are high because it’s seen as one of Democrats’ last assured opportunities to redraw new congressional lines before November.

In Maryland, where Democrats hold a 7-1 edge, Gov. Wes Moore’s (D) Redistricting Advisory Commission is set to vote on a new congressional map proposal, though it still requires legislative approval. The party is looking to weaken Rep. Andy Harris’s (R-Md.) district in the Eastern Shore. Democrats tried once before to create an aggressive 8-0 map after the 2020 U.S. Census, only to see it shot down as a partisan gerrymander by a court.

Maryland Senate President Bill Ferguson (D) has also been vocally opposed to drawing a new map, raising questions of whether Democrats even have the numbers in both chambers to pass a new map. If they do, it’s not clear it would withstand litigation from Republicans again.

Florida’s expected special session on redistricting in April also is adding to the urgency for Democrats.

However, Republicans must navigate a few hurdles of their own, including restrictive anti-gerrymandering language included in the state Constitution and potentially differing timelines between Gov. Ron DeSantis (R) and Florida House Republicans of when to draw a new map.

data.title: 'An incredibly harsh environment': Why seizing Greenland doesn't mean securing its minerals
data.description:
Greenland holds large quantities of rare earth elements, but experts say annexing the territory may not vastly improve U.S. access to them. 

President Trump has dug in on his efforts to take over Greenland, which is part of Denmark, a NATO ally. Despite vocal objections from leaders across Europe, Trump has insisted it is important for the U.S. to take possession of the island, refusing to rule out the use of the military to do so.

Asked Tuesday during a press conference to mark his first full year back in office how far he was willing to go to acquire Greenland, the president told reporters: “You’ll find out.”

While Trump also recently said, “We need Greenland for national security, not minerals,” some of his allies are eyeing both the territory’s location and its natural resources.

“Greenland has massive rare earth minerals and critical minerals. There are enormous economic benefits to America,” Sen. Ted Cruz (R-Texas) said on Fox News’s “Sunday Morning Futures With Maria Bartiromo” over the weekend.

Trump adviser Mike Waltz similarly said last year that the administration’s push for Greenland is “about critical minerals. ”

Greenland has significant quantities of rare earth elements — minerals that can be used in various technologies, including semiconductors, batteries, lasers, magnets and nuclear energy. Ultimately, such technologies can be used in everything from data centers to military weapons to renewable power and electric vehicles.

These minerals aren’t actually exceedingly rare, they just occur in small deposits and can therefore be uneconomic to mine.

The International Energy Agency (IEA), whose members include major energy consuming countries, has projected that by 2050, global demand for rare earth elements used in magnets will double. It said that China is expected to remain their largest supplier. 

According to the Center for Strategic and International Studies, Greenland has two of the largest deposits of rare earths in the world, but the conditions there — particularly the fact the island is largely covered in ice — pose a challenge for actually mining those deposits.

And that is just one of the hurdles.

“Greenland is large. Some of these mining sites are quite remote. They require people with specialized skills … to access these minerals, and most of these places are not settled and not near places where people live,” said Penny Naas, senior vice president for innovation and competitiveness at the German Marshall Fund of the United States, a think tank that promotes transatlantic cooperation. 

“There’s still … other places around the world that are easier to mine” Naas added, also noting that mining in Greenland often faces local opposition.

And, she said, an American takeover may not actually yield different results for companies than just working with existing authorities.

“Greenland is relatively open on investment,” Naas said. “In general, right now the U.S. could go in and mine in Greenland as is.”

“There’s only one U.S. entity that has applied for and received mining permits in Greenland. The U.K. recently got one. There’s a lot of Canadians and Australians that have mining permits. But … the challenge is not so much going in and getting it. It’s that it’s just an incredibly harsh environment to mine in,” she added. 

China controls much of the world’s mineral supply chains. The country accounts for 61 percent of global rare earth element mining and more than 90 percent of rare earth element refining, according to the 2025 IEA report. 

However, over the course of the past year, the U.S. government has set up deals with miners to produce rare earths domestically. 

Ian Lange, who was part of the White House Council of Economic Advisers during Trump’s first term, noted that companies were working toward “vertically integrated supply chain[s]” while Greenland is “totally away from the supply chain.”

“Sure, there’s dirt there that has the minerals that people talk about in it, but my general sense is: So does the U.S., so does Canada, so does Australia, so does Brazil,” said Lange, who is now an economics and business professor at the Colorado School of Mines.

He said the markets may not have a need for Greenland’s minerals — comparing it to an area with too many grocery stores.

“Greenland’s like a Whole Foods. We’ve got Trader Joe’s, we’ve got Kroger. We’ve got …17 grocery stores near us and somebody says, ‘Hey, let’s put in an 18th,’” he said. “Greenland would be the 18th grocery store.”

data.title: Air Force One to land at Joint Base Andrews after crew finds 'minor electrical issue'
data.description:
Air Force One on Tuesday, en route to the World Economic Forum in Davos, Switzerland, will land at Joint Base Andrews after crew members identified “a minor electrical issue.”

White House press secretary Karoline Leavitt confirmed that the issue was found after takeoff and, out of an abundance of caution, Air Force One will turn around and land at the base before taking off in a different aircraft.

Leavitt joked that the Qatari jet given to the U.S. to replace Air Force One in May was sounding “much better” now.

Trump accepted the gift jet, a $400 million luxury Boeing 747-8 that the Qatari royal family had previously used. Chief Pentagon spokesperson Sean Parnell previously said the Department of Defense will “work to ensure proper security measures and functional-mission requirements are considered for an aircraft used to transport the President of the United States.”

The president and his Cabinet are expected to meet with European leaders in Davos amid Trump’s escalating rhetoric over the U.S. acquiring Greenland. On Saturday, Trump slapped 10 percent tariffs on Denmark and other European allies until the U.S. acquires the island territory.

data.title: Bondi announces departure of Halligan from US Attorneys office
data.description:
Attorney General Pam Bondi on Tuesday announced that Lindsey Halligan, the top prosecutor in the Eastern District of Virginia, has departed her position, citing Senate Democrats “making it impossible” for her to continue past her 120-day appointment deadline.

“During her 120-day tenure as Interim United States Attorney for the Eastern District of Virginia, Lindsey Halligan served with the utmost distinction and an unwavering commitment to the rule of law,” Bondi said in a statement posted on the social platform X.

Bondi accused Senate Democrats of weaponizing the “blue slip process, making it impossible for Lindsey’s term as United States Attorney to continue following the expiration of her 120-day appointment.”

Halligan’s departure “is a significant loss for the Department of Justice and the communities she served,” Bondi said. “While we feel her absence keenly, we are confident that she will continue to serve her country in other ways.”

“The circumstances that led to this outcome are deeply misguided,” Bondi continued in closing. “We are living in a time when a democratically elected President’s ability to staff key law enforcement positions faces serious obstacles. The Department of Justice will continue to seek review of decisions like this that hinder our ability to keep the American people safe.”

Earlier on Tuesday, Chief District Judge M. Hannah Lauck posted a job opening for interim U.S. attorney for the Eastern District of Virginia. He entered an order stating that Halligan “may not serve” in her previous role for more than 120 days.

Bondi tapped Halligan after President Trump’s previous U.S. attorney nominee Erik Siebert refused to investigate New York Attorney General Letitia James (D) in connection with mortgage fraud. Halligan launched inquiries into James and former FBI Director James Comey regarding allegations that they lied during congressional testimony.

Halligan defended her position after U.S. District Judge Cameron Currie ruled in November that she was ineligible to stay in her role and dismissed the cases against James and Comey. Currie argued that her appointment expired while Siebert was U.S. attorney and ruled that she was not eligible to be the district’s top federal prosecutor.

U.S. District Judge David Novak ordered Halligan on Jan. 6 to give an explanation of why she continued her duties despite Currie’s order.

“The bottom line is that Ms. Halligan has not ‘misrepresented’ anything and the Court is flat wrong to suggest that any change to the Government’s signature block is warranted in this or any other case,” an 11-page filing to Novak read.

Halligan was one of several of Trump’s U.S. attorneys to be disqualified, with the top prosecutors in New Jersey, the Northern District of New York, Nevada and the district in California covering Los Angeles all having been disqualified. A challenge is pending against a prosecutor in New Mexico.

data.title: Trump: Anything Europe does to threaten US on trade 'I'll just meet it'
data.description:
President Trump said Tuesday in an interview with The Hill’s sister network NewsNation that he will match Europe if it moves to threaten the U.S. on trade amid rising tensions over Greenland. 

“See anything they do with us, I’ll just meet it. All I have to do is meet it, and it’s going to go ricocheting backward,” Trump told NewsNation’s Katie Pavlich on “Katie Pavlich Tonight.” 

“But we’re not looking at that. We’ll probably be able to work something out, possibly even during the next few days in Davos,” he continued.

The interview comes as Trump travels to Davos, Switzerland for the World Economic Forum where he’ll come face to face with a number of European allies who have raised concerns of his vocal ambitions for the U.S. to acquire Greenland. 

Over the weekend, Trump levied 10 percent tariffs on Denmark and its European allies over Greenland. Additionally, the president has not ruled out military force to acquire the territory. 

UK Prime Minister Keir Starmer issued a rare criticism of Trump over the issue, saying at a press conference earlier that “any decision about the future status of Greenland belongs to the people of Greenland and the kingdom of Denmark alone.”

Trump on Tuesday also shared a screenshot of a conversation with French President Emmanuel Macron in which Macron tells him, “I do not understand what you are doing on Greenland.” 

However, Trump appeared optimistic at Tuesday’s press briefing, predicting a deal would be made on Greenland at Davos. 

“I think we’re going to work something out where NATO’s going to be very happy and we’re going to be very happy,” Trump said.

data.title: Trump signs executive order barring Wall Street investors from buying single-family homes
data.description:
President Trump on Tuesday signed an executive order stopping Wall Street investors from buying and owning single-family homes, amid his push to focus on affordability ahead of the November midterm elections.

“Buying and owning a home has long been considered the pinnacle of the American dream and a way for families to invest and build lifetime wealth,” Trump’s order reads. “But because of the recent high inflation and interest rates caused by the previous administration, that American dream has been increasingly out of reach for too many of our citizens, especially first-time homebuyers.”

Trump said “large” Wall Street investors have bought a “growing share of single-family homes,” creating competition with “hardworking young families.

“Neighborhoods and communities once controlled by middle-class American families are now run by faraway corporate interests,” Trump continued. “People live in homes, not corporations.  My Administration will take decisive action to stop Wall Street from treating America’s neighborhoods like a trading floor and empower American families to own their homes.”

The order stated that in 30 days, Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent will develop “definitions of ‘large institutional investor’ and ‘single-family home.'” Cabinet members will issue guidance to prevent “providing for, approving, insuring, guaranteeing, securitizing, or facilitating the acquisition by large institutional investors” of a single-family home.

Attorney General Pam Bondi and Federal Trade Commission Chair Andrew Ferguson will review substantial acquisitions by heavy investors “and prioritize enforcement of the antitrust laws, as appropriate, against coordinated vacancy and pricing strategies by large institutional investors in local single-family home rental markets,” the order reads.

Trump called for the ban earlier this month, writing on Truth Social that he was “taking steps” to implement it, adding that he “will be calling on Congress to codify it.”

“For a very long time, buying and owning a home was considered the pinnacle of the American Dream,” he wrote. “It was the reward for working hard, and doing the right thing, but now, because of the Record High Inflation caused by Joe Biden and the Democrats in Congress, that American Dream is increasingly out of reach for far too many people, especially younger Americans.”

Many real estate trusts and private equity firms have collected a considerable number of single-family homes ultimately used for rent, destabilizing the homebuying market.

Trump and Republicans in Congress have focused on affordability ahead of the midterms to tell voters that inflation has fallen and prices have improved. Americans largely do not believe conditions have improved, which Trump acknowledged on Tuesday that the administration might have “bad public relations people” selling his economic achievements.

data.title: Healthcare group projects anti-RFK Jr. messages onto HHS building
data.description:
Protect Our Care, a healthcare advocacy group, on Tuesday projected two critical messages onto the facade of the Health and Human Services (HHS) building in Washington, D.C., blaming HHS Secretary Robert F. Kennedy Jr. for policies they say are making Americans sicker.

“RFK JR: MAKING AMERICA SICK AGAIN,” read one message, projected outside the building.

Another message read: “MEASLES HQ.”

Kayla Hancock, director of Public Health Watch at Protect Our Care, told The Hill that the messages aim to raise awareness of the resurgence of Measles and to push the Trump administration to act with greater urgency to encourage vaccination.

 “Secretary Kennedy has been busy doing anything and everything but addressing or even acknowledging the worsening measles outbreak that is almost exclusively harming unvaccinated children,” Hancock said.

“We hope that shining a literal spotlight on the HHS will spur the Trump administration to raise awareness of the proven safety and efficacy of the measles vaccine,” she continued. “It’s the least RFK Jr. can do after he helped cause this mess by spreading dangerous, fabricated lies about the shot that likely scared many parents out of vaccinating their children.”

The messages on the HHS building came the same day the U.S. met a key condition required to lose its measles elimination status, which the country has had for more than 25 years.

The Pan American Health Organization (PAHO), the World Health Organization’s (WHO) regional office for the Americas, considers Jan. 20, 2025, the date marking the beginning of consistent measles spread in the U.S. 

The Centers for Disease Control and Prevention (CDC), however, said it’s too soon to declare an end to the country’s measles elimination status, noting that the exact chain of transmission must first be determined.

Ralph Abraham, a former Louisiana surgeon general who was sworn in as the CDC’s principal deputy director last month, said it’s unclear whether the West Texas outbreak in January is related to the ongoing spread in states such as South Carolina, which has reported more than 600 cases since October.  

Protect Our Care held a virtual news conference earlier Tuesday, when Hawaii Gov. Josh Green (D), who is also a physician, said the Trump administration “laid the foundation for mistrust and distrust of vaccinations over the course of this year, and … now you’re seeing the terrible outcomes.”

“What’s happening today with Measles is absolutely public health malpractice, and it’s completely preventable,” Green said earlier Tuesday.

“It’s unconscionable that HHS is allowing this to happen,” he added. “I don’t criticize them for everything that they do, but this, more than anything, is a tragedy, because it’s completely, completely preventable.”

data.title: Georgia state Senate race heads to runoff
data.description:
The race to fill a state Senate seat in Georgia is headed to a runoff, Decision Desk HQ has projected.

Democrat Lemario Brown and Republican Steven McNeel will head to a Feb. 17 runoff after neither candidate was able to cross the 50 percent threshold. There were five Republicans running, splitting the vote and helping Brown advance in a reliably red district.

The Peach State’s 18th state Senate district became vacant when the incumbent, Republican state Sen. John F. Kennedy (R), resigned last month to focus on his campaign for lieutenant governor, triggering a special election.

Republicans are favored to win the seat, where Kennedy won reelection uncontested in 2024.

Republicans hold a 32-23 majority in the Georgia state Senate, which gaveled in this month and is expected to wrap up in late March or early April, according to the Georgia Recorder. 

The race comes after two special elections for the state legislature in the last two months headed to runoffs. A Democrat won a Georgia Senate seat in December, and a Republican won a Georgia House seat in January.

Democrats also flipped two seats on the Georgia Public Service Commission and flipped a state House seat in a red-leaning district last year, adding to blue momentum ahead of the midterms. 

The winner of the special election would have to run again next fall to win a full term in the state chamber.

data.title: Home sellers outnumber buyers by widest margin on record, Redfin says
data.description:
(NewsNation) —  A few years ago, buyers were battling for homes. Now, they’re the ones with leverage.

In December, there were 47% more home sellers than buyers — roughly 631,000 extra — the largest gap in records going back to 2013, according to new estimates from Redfin.

The widening gap reflects a sharp drop in the number of active buyers, which fell to 1.34 million last month, the lowest level in Redfin’s 12 years of data. Buyers haven’t been this scarce since April 2020 at the start of the pandemic.

“Homebuyers are backing off due to stubbornly high home prices and mortgage rates, layoffs, and mounting economic and political uncertainty,” Redfin noted.

It’s a stark reversal from 2021, when bidding wars were routine and buyers outnumbered sellers. The two sides were roughly even as recently as Feb. 2024, but since then, the number of sellers has consistently outpaced buyers.

The imbalance is most visible in the Sun Belt, where pandemic boomtowns fueled a wave of new construction that added supply and helped slow price growth. Demand has also cooled after years of steep price gains.

In Austin, there were an estimated 128% more home sellers than buyers in December — the widest gap among the top 50 metros. Fort Lauderdale (125%), Nashville (111%), Miami (103%) and San Antonio (103%) also posted large imbalances, Redfin said.

Part of the challenge for sellers is that many who bought near the peak of the pandemic market have been slow to reset their expectations.

Connie Durnal, a Redfin Premier agent in the Dallas area, said some clients are adjusting to the new reality, while others are not: “A lot of sellers are in denial and won’t budge on price.”

“If you don’t price your home reasonably, it will sit on the market,” Durnal warned in the report.

In the Northeast and Midwest — regions that have historically issued the fewest building permits — the picture looks different. Sellers continue to have the upper hand and prices are rising faster than the national average in several markets.

Buyers outnumber sellers by the widest margin in Nassau County, NY, followed by Montgomery County, PA and Newark, NJ. Milwaukee, WI, and New Brunswick, N,J also had more buyers than sellers last month.

Overall, 36 of the 50 most populous metros were considered buyer’s markets in December, meaning they had at least 10% more sellers than buyers, Redfin said. By comparison, just give metros qualified as sellers’ markets.

The growing divide between buyers and sellers underscores how weak the housing market remained in 2025, with existing-home sales hovering near a 30-year low.

Long-term mortgage rates have eased recently, hitting their lowest level in more than three years, but at 6.06% the average 30-year fixed is still roughly double where it stood in 2021.

Forecasters don’t expect mortgage rates to fall much further in 2026, despite actions by President Donald Trump aimed at lowering borrowing costs.

The good news for prospective buyers is that the so-called “lock-in effect” has been easing, with the share of homeowners holding sub-3% rates declining. That shift could help improve inventory in the months and years ahead.

Redfin has dubbed 2026 “The Great Housing Reset,” calling it the start of a “long, slow recovery” in a recent forecast.

Redfin estimates the number of active buyers using proprietary data on the typical time between a buyer’s first home tour and closing, along with MLS data on active listings and pending sales.

Top 10 buyer’s markets in the U.S. in Dec. 2025 (Redfin data for top 50 metros)

Percent by which sellers outnumber buyers

  1. Austin, TX: 128.4%
  2. Fort Lauderdale, FL: 125.1%
  3. Nashville, TN: 111.3%
  4. Miami, FL: 102.7%
  5. San Antonio, TX: 102.5%
  6. West Palm Beach, FL: 97.4%
  7. Houston, TX: 96.6%
  8. Las Vegas, NV: 88.9%
  9. Dallas, TX: 86.8%
  10. Tampa, FL: 82.2%
